claim.hypotheses_location: The study discusses and tests several hypotheses in the section that compares UNIFORM and FIXED treatments (approx. middle of the paper, pp. 6–8).
claim.statement_location: The core claim that uniform price auctions raise higher revenues than fixed-price offerings is repeated in the Abstract and again in the Conclusions section (last two pages of the paper).

data.unit_of_analysis: Individual bidder (per round) and auction round
data.notes: Groups contained 3 informed and 1 uninformed bidder; each session lasted 20 rounds.

method.description: Subjects, divided into groups of four (three informed and one uninformed), participated in 20-round laboratory sessions that implemented either a uniform-price auction (UNIFORM) or a fixed-price offering (FIXED). Signals and fundamental values were randomized each round. Participants bid for a fixed supply of shares; earnings were converted to cash. Post-experiment data were analysed with non-parametric tests to compare prices, revenues and pay-offs across treatments.
steps: Recruit subjects and assign each to one of 14 four-person groups, maintaining informed/uninformed roles.
Randomly assign each group to either the UNIFORM or FIXED treatment.
In each of 20 rounds: draw the market value, distribute private signals (H or L) to informed bidders, and allow bidding according to the treatment rules.
Determine allocations and market prices (endogenous in UNIFORM, fixed at 1.94 in FIXED) and record pay-offs.
Convert experimental points to money and pay subjects; ensure no subject ends with negative earnings (minimum payment guaranteed).
Aggregate round-level data and conduct statistical analysis (Spearman rank correlations, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests) of market prices, revenues, bidder pay-offs, and underpricing.
method.models: Non-parametric statistical tests; comparison to theoretical equilibria (TCE, EH, EHL, EHU, EHLU).
method.outcome_variable: Seller revenue (and related market price); bidder pay-offs; degree of underpricing.
method.independent_variables: Auction mechanism (UNIFORM vs FIXED), bidder signal type (H, L, U), market value.

results.summary: Uniform price auctions generated significantly higher market prices and seller revenues than fixed-price offerings. Average market prices in UNIFORM were above those in FIXED (p = 0.001) and not significantly different from the unconditional expected value of 2.5 (p = 0.080). Seller revenues were correspondingly higher in UNIFORM (p = 0.001). Underpricing occurred less frequently and was less severe in UNIFORM. High-signal bidders bid more aggressively than low-signal and uninformed bidders, and no evidence of tacit collusion equilibria (TCE) was observed.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Difference in average market price (UNIFORM − FIXED)
